nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving blackwater streams and flooded igapó forest pools in the Cenepa River basin, an upper tributary of the Marañón system on the Peru–Ecuador border. Waters are extremely soft and acidic, stained brown with tannins, and rich in leaf litter and submerged roots, with little to no current.

decorPreferences: dark substrate (black sand or fine dark gravel)
dried Indian almond leaves and mixed leaf litter
driftwood branches and tangled roots for cover
floating plants (Amazon frogbit, water lettuce) to diffuse lighting
dense fine-leaved planting (Java moss, Cryptocoryne, Riccia)
blackwater tannins from peat filtration, alder cones, or commercial blackwater extract

diet.notes: In the wild, the Cenepa Red Pencilfish feeds on tiny invertebrates, zooplankton, micro-worms, and algal biofilm. In the aquarium it generally accepts high-quality micro-pellets and micro-granules (no larger than about 0.8 mm), finely crushed flake, frozen or live baby brine shrimp (Artemia nauplii), micro worms, Daphnia, and Cyclops. Its mouth is very small, so food particles should be kept within roughly 0.5–1.0 mm. Regular live or frozen foods are important for conditioning, immune support in soft acidic water, and full colour development.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras tolerant of acid blackwater (e.g., cardinal tetras, ember tetras)
dwarf corydoras (e.g., Corydoras pygmaeus, C. habrosus)
small Apistogramma species (with caution — provide dense cover)
Caridina and Neocaridina shrimp in species or biotope setups
avoidWith: any fish large enough to swallow them
boisterous or fast-swimming fish that outcompete at feeding
fish requiring hard alkaline water (incompatible chemistry)

summary: An undescribed Nannostomus from the Cenepa River basin (Peru/Ecuador) circulating in the trade as ‘Cenepa Red.’ Males display intense red body coloration closely resembling the Coral Red Pencilfish (N. mortenthaleri, fw-fish-169) — the two species are frequently confused in the trade. Demands extreme soft acid blackwater (pH 4.5–6.0) and is suited only for experienced blackwater specialists.
careNotes: Nannostomus sp. ‘Cenepa Red’ is a trade designation for an as-yet undescribed pencilfish collected from the Cenepa River drainage, a Marañón tributary straddling the Peru–Ecuador border. It has not received a formal scientific description and should be treated as a nominal species pending publication. Confusion with the Coral Red Pencilfish (Nannostomus mortenthaleri, fw-fish-169) is extremely common: both species share vivid red coloration in males, originate from Amazonian Peru, and occupy identical blackwater biotopes. The Cenepa Red is generally distinguished by a slightly more intense all-over red flush, finer white lateral stripe contrast, and its distinct Cenepa River provenance versus N. mortenthaleri’s Rio Nanay origin — but visual separation in trade stock is unreliable and vendors frequently mislabel them. Aquarists should assume either species demands identical care. Water chemistry is non-negotiable: pH must be held between 4.5 and 6.0; values above 6.5 will suppress coloration and compromise long-term health. Hardness (gH) should target 0–4 dGH using reverse-osmosis water buffered with peat, Indian almond leaves, alder cones, or commercial blackwater extracts. Conductivity below 100 µS/cm is ideal. Filtration must be gentle — sponge filters are strongly preferred; powerhead-driven flow will stress the fish. Lighting should be dim; floating plants and tannin-stained water are both essential for the species to feel secure and display full coloration. Keep in groups of 8 or more: males perform lateral displays and show heightened coloration in the presence of rivals and females. A balanced sex ratio reduces inter-male tension. All specimens are wild-caught imports from Peru or Ecuador; drip-acclimate slowly and quarantine for a minimum of 4 weeks in matching soft acid water before introducing to a display tank. Do not use standard pH-neutral quarantine conditions.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has been reported by specialist keepers but remains uncommon and poorly documented for this undescribed form. Protocol mirrors that for N. mortenthaleri: condition the group for 2–4 weeks on live Artemia nauplii and micro worms, then transfer a conditioned pair or small group to a 20–40 L breeding tank with RO water (pH 5.0–5.5, gH 1–2 dGH), gentle sponge filtration, and dense Java moss or fine spawning mops. Eggs are scattered among fine-leaved plants and receive no parental care; adults will eat eggs if not removed promptly. Eggs hatch in approximately 24–36 hours at 26°C. Fry are very small and require infusoria or commercially prepared first foods (e.g., Sera Micron) before graduating to Artemia nauplii at 5–7 days. Breeding difficulty is rated advanced due to strict water chemistry demands, tiny fry size, and the near-total reliance on wild-caught parent stock.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Nannostomus mortenthaleri (comparative basis)
primary.notes: Primary reference used for water parameters, biotope, and husbandry requirements shared with the closely related N. mortenthaleri; Nannostomus sp. ‘Cenepa Red’ is undescribed and has no dedicated SeriouslyFish page. Parameters (pH 4.0–6.0, temperature 23–28°C, hardness 0–5 dGH), native blackwater biotope, diet, and breeding approach drawn from this account and applied to the Cenepa form with adjustments for its distinct river-basin provenance.

additional.name: FishBase — Nannostomus mortenthaleri
additional.notes: Cross-check for taxonomy (Lebiasinidae, Characiformes), adult size (max 3.5 cm TL), and native range data applicable to the sister group. Nannostomus sp. ‘Cenepa Red’ is absent from FishBase as it has not been formally described.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Pencilfish Care Guide
additional.notes: General pencilfish trade guidance: group size recommendations (8+), micro-food feeding strategy, subdued lighting and leaf litter setup, and quarantine approach. No dedicated page for the Cenepa Red form; guidance applied from general Nannostomus husbandry.

additional.name: Aquarium Glaser — Nannostomus sp. Cenepa Red import listing
additional.notes: German wholesale importer listing for Nannostomus sp. ‘Cenepa Red’; confirms trade existence of this undescribed form, Cenepa River provenance, and provides photographs of live trade specimens. Used to confirm the fish’s status as a distinct trade form separate from N. mortenthaleri.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Nannostomus mortenthaleri profile
additional.notes: Cross-check of water parameter extremes, blackwater biotope requirements, confusion between red pencilfish forms, wild collection notes, and minimum group size guidance applicable to the closely related Cenepa Red.

fish.breedingNotes: The Cenepa Red Pencilfish is an egg scatterer. Breeding requires a dedicated tank of 20–40 litres filled with RO water at pH 5.0–5.5 and gH 1–2 dGH, equipped with gentle sponge filtration and dense fine-leaved plants or spawning mops. Adults will eat their eggs and must be removed promptly after spawning. Fry are very small and should be started on infusoria or prepared first foods before graduating to Artemia nauplii at around five to seven days.
